verses.text: “Be careful not to practice your righteousness before people to be seen by them, for then indeed you will have no reward from your Father who is in heaven.
Therefore, whenever you give to the poor, do not sound a trumpet as the hypocrites do in synagogues and in the streets to be praised by people. Amen I tell you, they have received their reward.
But when you give to the poor, do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing,
so that your giving will be in secret, and your Father who sees what is done in secret will reward you.

And whenever you pray, do not be like hypocrites who love to stand and pray in the synagogues and on street corners before people to be seen by them. Amen I tell you, they have received their reward in full.
But whenever you pray, go into your private room, shut the door, and pray to your Father in secret, and your Father who sees what is done in secret will reward you.”
“When you pray, do not repeat mindlessly like the Gentiles, for they think by speaking many words, they will be heard.
Therefore, do not be like them, for your Father knows what you need before you ask Him.
Thus pray like this: Our Father who is in heaven, holy is your name.
Your kingdom come, your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven.
Give us today our daily bread.
And forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors.
Lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one. [For Yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory forever. Amen.].
For if you forgive people their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you.
But if you do not forgive people, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses.”
“When you fast, do not become sad looking like the hypocrites, for they disfigure their faces to be seen fasting by people. Amen I tell you, they have received their reward.
But when you fast, anoint your head and wash your face,
so that you may not appear to people as fasting, but to your Father in secret; and your Father who sees what is done in secret will reward you.
“Do not store up treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy, and where thieves break in and steal.
But store up treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor rust destroys, and where thieves do not break in or steal.
For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.”
The eye is the lamp of the body. If your eye is pure, your whole body will be full of light.
But if your eye is evil, your whole body will be full of darkness. If then the light you rely on is darkness, how deep is that darkness.

sections.headline: A Slave to God and Wealth

verses.text: No one can be a slave to two masters. Either you will hate one and love the other, or be devoted to one and despise the other. You cannot be a slave to God and Mammon.
“Therefore I tell you, do not worry about your life, what you will eat, nor for the body, and what you will wear. Is not life more than food, and the body more than clothing?
Look at the birds of the air. They do not sow, or reap, or gather into barns, yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not much more worthy than they?
Who among you by worrying can add a single hour to their lifetime?
And why do you worry about clothing? Consider how the lilies of the field grow; they do not labor or spin,
yet I tell you, not even Solomon in all his splendor was clothed like one of these.
If God so clothes the grass of the field, which is alive today and tomorrow is thrown in the oven, will He not much more clothe you? You of little faith?
Therefore, do not worry, saying, “What will we eat? What will we drink? or What will we wear?”
For the Gentiles chase these things, and your heavenly Father knows that you need all of these.
Seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness, and all these things will be added to you.
Therefore, do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will carry its own burdens. Each day carries enough of its own evil.”

footnotes.content: The phrase “sound a trumpet” is metaphorical, referring to showy, attention-seeking behavior. There is no evidence that literal trumpets were used in acts of charity. Jesus is condemning those who turn giving into public performance to gain praise. The phrase “they have their reward in full” translates the Greek apechousin ton misthon autōn; the verb apechō was used in financial contexts to indicate full payment received—meaning public praise is the only reward they will get, with nothing further from God.

The Greek phrase ho blepōn en tō kryptō means “the one who sees in secret.” The words “what is done” were added for clarity, making the implied action explicit in the phrase “who sees what is done in secret.”

The phrase “meaningless repetition” translates the Greek verb battalogēsēte, a rare term possibly derived from the onomatopoeic root battal-, suggesting stammering or babbling. While the precise etymology is uncertain, the meaning in context is clear: speaking with excessive, empty, or mechanical repetition. Jesus is not condemning repeated prayer—as seen in the Psalms or in his own prayers—but is warning against ritualistic or performance-driven speech, such as was common in pagan practices where word quantity was prioritized over heart sincerity. The expression “meaningless repetitions” captures this distinction with clarity for modern readers, avoiding confusion with genuine, heartfelt persistence in prayer.

The concluding phrase, “For Yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory forever. Amen,” does not appear in the earliest Greek manuscripts of Matthew but is included in many later Byzantine texts and became part of the church's liturgical tradition. It was likely added during public worship and later incorporated into manuscript copies. While it may not have been spoken by Jesus in this specific context, the doxology is theologically consistent with the message of the prayer and serves as a fitting closure of praise. It is retained here for its deep devotional impact and its long-standing use in Christian worship, in keeping with this translation's emphasis on spiritual resonance and reader edification.
“Pile up” renders thēsaurizō here negatively (greedy hoarding). It contrasts with the positive “store up … in heaven” in v. 20.

The Greek word haplous refers to an eye that is single-minded, sincere, and morally undivided. It implies integrity of purpose—a clear, generous, and undistracted vision. This is not about physical health, but about ethical clarity: seeing the world through a heart free from greed, lust, or double motives. Jesus contrasts this with the “evil eye” in the next verse, a Hebrew idiom for selfishness or envy (cf. Proverbs 28:22). A “pure” eye lets in the light of truth; a corrupt eye darkens the whole self.

The word “evil” translates the Greek ponēros, which refers to a morally and spiritually corrupt condition. Jesus is not merely speaking of sinful behavior, but of a distorted spiritual perception—when the “eye,” representing one's inner moral vision, is diseased. The result is that the entire person becomes darkened. The phrase “if the light you rely on is darkness” heightens the danger: it refers to those who believe they are enlightened, yet are profoundly self-deceived. The concluding cry, “how deep is that darkness,” is a Hebraic idiom expressing overwhelming moral blindness. It echoes Isaiah 5:20, where good is called evil and light is called darkness, underscoring the terrifying depth of spiritual delusion.

Wealth here translates the Greek word mamōnas transliterated as Mammon, originally from Aramaic. It refers not merely to money or possessions but to wealth as a spiritual power—personified as a rival master to God. Jesus uses Mammon to expose the heart's divided loyalties, showing that true devotion cannot be shared between God and materialism.

Jesus is not condemning planning or responsibility, but warning against anxious preoccupation with material needs. This verse follows his teaching that one cannot serve both God and wealth—the Greek word mamōnas, often personified to represent wealth as a spiritual rival. Worry, in this context, becomes a competing master—dividing the heart and pulling it away from trust in divine provision. By asking, “Is not life more than food…?” Jesus redirects attention to the God who gave life, affirming that the One who created the body can be trusted to sustain it. The command not to worry is not about passive detachment, but about active trust in a Father who knows and provides.

"His righteousness" refers to God's moral standard, justice, and covenant faithfulness. To seek His righteousness means to pursue a life aligned with God's will—both in outward actions and inward devotion—reflecting what is right and pleasing to Him.

Jesus teaches a posture of present-focused trust. “Tomorrow will carry its own burdens” means that future troubles will arrive in their time, and worrying now only adds unnecessary weight. Each day brings its own challenges; faith calls us to live one day at a time, trusting God for what lies ahead.
